title: [critic] Limiting argument: upper-semicontinuity direction and limsup/lim bookkeeping near multiple roots

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
The reduction from simple roots (where $\Phi_n$ is smooth) to the general case uses $T_\epsilon=(1-\epsilon\,d/dx)^n$ and a limit $\epsilon\to0$. There is a genuine DIRECTION subtlety that can silently break the proof.

**Key fact.** $\Phi_n$ is NOT continuous: it jumps to $+\infty$ at root coalescence. The correct property is *lower semicontinuity of $\Phi_n$*, equivalently *upper semicontinuity of $1/\Phi_n$*.

**Required.** (1) Prove $1/\Phi_n$ is upper semicontinuous on $\mathbb R^n$ (extended-real valued). (2) From (★) applied to the smoothed inputs (simple-rooted for $\epsilon>0$), take $\limsup_{\epsilon\to0}$ on the LHS $1/\Phi_n(\cdot)$ (valid by USC) and $\lim$ on the RHS; verify the inequality survives. (3) Crucially handle the case where $p$ or $q$ ALREADY has a multiple root: then the corresponding RHS term is $0$ and only $\limsup$ control (not continuity) is available; show this is enough.

**Verified numerically:** the degenerate equality case (one of $p,q$ an $n$-fold root) gives $\boxplus_n$ = pure translation, $1/\Phi_n(q)=0$, and (★) holds with equality — the limiting argument must reproduce this.

Complements human issue q4-49; this isolates the *semicontinuity direction + limsup/lim placement* as the correctness-critical deliverable.
